coredata.dc:title: Controllable functionalization of amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots as fluorescent probe for detection of Cu(Ⅱ) ions detection
coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Chunxiao
Tian, Chunlan
Cao, Wei
Zhang, Meng
Zhang, Taihu
Tang, Jianing
Zhang, Fan
Chen, Guo
Tang, Ju
coredata.dc:description: Herein, the amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can be applied for selective detection of Cu(Ⅱ) ions in solutions via photoluminescence (PL) measurement, the PL emission intensities of the GQDs gradually decreased with the increase of Cu2+ concentration (0–100 μm), the PL intensity (quenching effect) decreases almost linearly with increasing Cu2+ concentration. Its correlation coefficient R 2 is 0.9529. Quenching of the PL emission by the presence of copper(Ⅱ) ions is attributed to the surface functional groups attached to the GQDs, which can interact with metal ions so that the electrons in the excited state of the GQDs are transferred into the unoccupied orbits of Cu(Ⅱ) ions. Corresponding physical mechanism is proposed to gain an understanding about how the presence of the Copper(Ⅱ) ions can affect the PL emission from GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots
Photoluminescence
Copper(Ⅱ) ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (a), Particle size distribution diagram (b), HRTEM image (c), XPS spectra (d), spectra of C1s (e), N1s (f) and FT-IR (g) of GQDs.

PL spectra of GQDs (a), PL emission spectra for GQDs with Cu2+ ions (b), Relationship between PL intensity and Cu2+ ions (c). Absorption spectra of pure GQDs and GQDs + Cu2+ (d). Tauc curve of GQDs andGQDs + Cu2+ (e). Schematic illustration of electronic energy levels and electronic transition channels of pure GQDs (f). Photoelectron transfer process for GQDs + Cu2+ (g).
Coordination diagram of Cu2+ with hydroxyl, carbonyl, and amino functional groups on the surface of GQDs in (a), (b) and (c). Schematic diagram of GQDs quenching process as shown in (d).

head.ce:title: Controllable functionalization of amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots as fluorescent probe for detection of Cu(Ⅱ) ions detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yunnan Key Laboratory of Unmanned Autonomous System, School of Electrical and Information Technology, Yunnan Minzu University, Kunming 650500, Yunnan, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Yunnan Key Laboratory of Unmanned Autonomous System
School of Electrical and Information Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yunnan Key Laboratory of Unmanned Autonomous System, School of Electrical and Information Technology, Yunnan Minzu University, Kunming 650500, Yunnan, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, the amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can be applied for selective detection of Cu(Ⅱ) ions in solutions via photoluminescence (PL) measurement, the PL emission intensities of the GQDs gradually decreased with the increase of Cu2+ concentration (0–100 μm), the PL intensity (quenching effect) decreases almost linearly with increasing Cu2+ concentration. Its correlation coefficient R 2 is 0.9529. Quenching of the PL emission by the presence of copper(Ⅱ) ions is attributed to the surface functional groups attached to the GQDs, which can interact with metal ions so that the electrons in the excited state of the GQDs are transferred into the unoccupied orbits of Cu(Ⅱ) ions. Corresponding physical mechanism is proposed to gain an understanding about how the presence of the Copper(Ⅱ) ions can affect the PL emission from GQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), one of the zero-dimensional materials, have their own quantum dots structural properties and excellent properties similar to those of graphene [1–3]. It is well known that GQDs are widely used in the fields of detection, sensing, catalysis, cellular imaging, drug delivery, and environmental pollution control due to their excellent optical properties [4,5], low toxicity [6–8], fluorescence stability [9–11] and good biocompatibility [12], etc.

Zhou et al. [13] utilized GQDs with green fluorescence as probes for the detection of Fe3+ and found that the GQDs were highly selective for Fe3+ and less interfered by other metal ions, which was attributed to the specific coordination between Fe3+ and phenolic hydroxyl groups of GQDs. Chakraborti et al. [14] found that GQDs can serve as effective chemical sensing device for selective detection of Hg2+ in aqueous solution, and the adsorption of Hg2+ on the surface of GQDs was proved to be the only reason for the quenching of the fluorescence property of GQDs after several experiments. The above-related studies inspired us to explore more practical applications of GQDs and reveal its fluorescence sensing mechanism.

For one of the applications of these GQDs, we use them for selective and rapid detection of Cu(II) ions (or Cu2+ in solution). We detected Cu(II) ions in solution by standard photoluminescence (PL) measurements. The mechanism of inducing GQDs to inhibit PL emission was proposed, which explaining the reason for the quenching phenomenon of GQDs.

The GQDs in this article were purchased from Suzhou carbon-rich graphene technology Co., LTD. The TEM, UV–vis, XPS, FT-IR and PL techniques are employed to characterize the morphology, elementary composition, and optical properties of these GQDs.

CuCl2·2H2O was weighed and dissolved in water, transferred to a volumetric flask, and the volume was adjusted to 5 mL to prepare 0.1 M Cu2+ standard solution, which can be further diluted with deionized water to obtain the Cu2+ ionic solution with a concentration range of 0–100 μm.

We find that theseGQDs are almost uniform in size and with good dispersion (Fig. 1 (a)). In Fig. 1(b), the results suggest thatthe particle size distribution of these GQDs is 10–35 nm, and the average particle size is 21.31 nm. Fig. 1(c) and Fig. 1(d) depict the HRTEM image and XRD of this sample, the corresponding lattice spacing is about 0.223 nm, and the lattice fringes are unambiguous. In Fig. 1(e), the full-scan XPS photoelectron spectra of the GQDs show that the atomic percentages of C1s, O1s, and N1s are 37.18 %, 30.38 %, and 2.74 %, respectively. The three response peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.3 eV, and 288.1 eV of the high-resolution spectrum of C1s shown in Fig. 1(f) correspond to the chemical bonds C-C/C = C, C-O, and O-C = O, respectively. In Fig. 1(g), the high-resolution spectrum of N1s of GQDs shows two typical peaks corresponding to graphitic nitrogen (N-C3, 399.8 eV) and an amine group (C-NH2, 401.7 eV), respectively. The chemical states and surface compositions of the GQDs were further confirmed using FT-IR, as shown in Fig. 2 (h), where the absorption bands at about 3500 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching of –OH. The characteristic absorption vibrational bands of the chemical bonds C-C/C = C and C-O are in the vicinity of 1640 and 1990 cm−1, respectively, and the absorption bands at 2360 and 1390 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of the N-C3 and C-NH2 bonds, respectively. In addition, a C-H stretching vibration is exhibited at 669 cm−1, which agrees with the XPS results. It indicates that the GQDs have carbon groups as the backbone and the surface articulation of oxygen- or nitrogen-containing characteristic functional groups such as C-O, C = O, and C-NH2.

Fig. 3 show the coordination diagrams of Cu2+ with hydroxyl, carbonyl, and amino functional groups on the surface/edge. In Fig. 3(a), the hydroxyl group has a stronger coupling activity, and its electron pair is more readily populated in the d-orbitals of the Cu2+ ions than the other negatively charged functional groups. In addition, the molecular orbitals of the carbonyl group are 6σ, 2p, 5σ, 1p, 4σ, and 3σ. Therefore, the molecular orbital that most easily forms structural coordination with metal ions is 5σ (Fig. 3(b)). Furthermore, there is an arc-electron pair on the amino group attached to the benzene ring, which cooperates with the Cu2+ ion to form a five-membered ring (Fig. 3(c)). The UV absorption spectra (Fig. 2(d)) of GQDs were tested to show that the surface state of GQDs is the primary channel for their photoluminescence. In addition, in the photoluminescence system of GQDs, the surface state of GQDs is not the only luminescence center of GQDs, and the PL emission of GQDs may also depend on the energy level jump between intrinsic eigenstates, as shown in Fig. 3(d). Under the action of photo-pumping, the electrons in the excited state can pass through the non-radiative electron-leaping channel relaxation to the unfilled d-orbitals of Cu2+ ions, and the PL emission of GQDs is suppressed. Therefore, the quenching phenomenon of Cu2+ by GQDs may result from a combination of coordination and dynamic quenching processes.

The amino-functionalized GQDs can be applied for selective detection of Cu(Ⅱ) ions in solutions, and the PL emission intensity of GQDs shows a linear decreasing trend with the increase of Cu2+ ion concentration. Further theoretical and computational studies show that the fluorescence quenching phenomenon is due to the coordination between GQDs and Cu2+ on the one hand and the dynamic quenching caused by the non-radiative transition of the excited electrons on the other hand. This study not only proves that the GQDs can be used as a promising probe for the rapid detection of Cu2+ but also provides a more detailed explanation of the fluorescence quenching mechanism.

Chunxiao Ren: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Chunlan Tian: Data curation. Wei Cao: Data curation. Meng Zhang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Taihu Zhang: Data curation. Jianing Tang: Investigation. Fan Zhang: Visualization, Supervision. Guo Chen: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Ju Tang: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 62063035), Yunnan Provincial Science and Technology Plan Project (No. 202301AU070127).
